title: Applying French polish to a small piece of furniture
subtitle: Build a shellac finish on a wooden surface using a polishing rubber and methylated spirit
excerpt: How to apply a French polish finish to a small piece of furniture using a shellac-charged rubber, covering making the rubber, applying bodying coats in circular and figure-of-eight passes, and finishing with straight grain strokes.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (furniture). The Workshop Companion (1913). Practical Furniture Restoration (1920s). The Practical Upholsterer (1917).

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of shellac flakes dissolved in methylated spirit, expressed in pounds per gallon. A one-pound cut (1 lb of flakes per gallon of spirit) is a thin solution used for the first sealing coats. A two-pound cut is the standard bodying mixture. Dissolve the flakes over 24 hours with occasional stirring; filter through muslin before use.

The pad used to apply French polish. It has two parts: an inner core of cotton-wool wadding charged with shellac solution, and an outer wrapper of clean lint-free linen or cotton cloth. The rubber is charged by opening the wrapper, adding shellac to the wadding, and rewrapping. It applies a thin film of shellac with each pass, building the finish in many fine layers.

content.text: finish applied in many thin layers with a

. Each pass deposits a fraction of a millimetre of shellac; the depth of finish builds over many sessions. The skill is in the rubber movement: circular passes during bodying, straight grain passes to finish. Work in a dust-free, warm room (above 15°C): cold or humid conditions cause the finish to bloom white.

Dewaxed blonde shellac flakes dissolved in methylated spirit at a two-pound cut (dissolve the flakes the day before use). Cotton-wool wadding and a square of clean, tightly woven cotton cloth for the rubber. Linseed oil: a single drop on the rubber face acts as a lubricant and is expelled during the finishing stage. Fine sanding sealer or a one-pound shellac cut for the first sealing coats on bare timber. Silicone-free fine grey scotch-brite or 400 grit paper for flatting between sessions.

Sand the bare timber to 180 grit, working with the grain. Seal the grain with a thin coat of one-pound shellac solution applied with a brush. Allow to dry for 30 minutes, then flat lightly with 320 grit to remove raised grain. Wipe with a clean cloth to remove all sanding dust. The surface should feel smooth with no rough patches before the rubber touches it.
Step 2, charge and test the rubber

Open the cloth wrapper and press a generous amount of two-pound shellac into the wadding. Rewrap the cloth and fold the ends under. Press the rubber face against a piece of clean glass: it should leave a trace of shellac that dries quickly. If it leaves a wet smear, the rubber is overcharged: squeeze the excess into a jar. Add a single drop of linseed oil to the rubber face with a fingertip. The oil prevents the rubber from sticking to the work surface.

Work across the surface in overlapping circular or figure-of-eight passes, keeping the rubber moving at all times. Never stop the rubber on the surface: lift it off at the end of each pass in a smooth sweeping motion. Apply five to six passes, then allow 10 minutes for the shellac to harden before the next round. Re-charge the rubber when it runs dry. After three to four sessions, the surface will have a visible depth of finish.

The final stage removes the linseed oil and straightens the surface. Charge the rubber with a diluted shellac (half spirit, half two-pound cut). Make long straight strokes along the grain from one end of the piece to the other, overlapping each stroke. The oil lifts to the surface and is carried off by the spirit in the rubber. Finish with two or three passes along the full length: the surface should look clear, bright, and grain-following.

Garnet shellac (a darker grade) gives a warm amber finish on mahogany and walnut. Blonde dewaxed shellac is the choice for lighter timbers (oak, ash, maple). Button shellac has a warm orange tone suited to pine and fruitwood. All grades dissolve and apply the same way; the grade changes only the colour of the dried film.

French polish is not heat or water resistant: do not use it on dining table tops or kitchen surfaces. It is the correct finish for period furniture, musical instruments, and display pieces kept away from heat and damp. A damaged area of French polish can be touched in with a fine brush charged with the same shellac cut, then blended with the rubber in the same way as the original application.

attrs.symptom: Finish blooms white in patches after the rubber is used
attrs.cause: The room is too cold or humid: the solvent in the shellac is chilling the surface and condensing moisture into the film.
attrs.fix: Warm the room to above 18°C and reduce humidity before continuing. Flatten the bloomed area with 400 grit and rebuild with fresh bodying coats in warmer conditions.

attrs.symptom: Rubber drags and pulls at the surface rather than gliding
attrs.cause: The rubber is overcharged with shellac and is depositing too thick a film, or the linseed oil has run out.
attrs.fix: Add another drop of oil to the rubber face. If the drag persists, the rubber is too wet: squeeze out excess shellac into the jar and retest against glass before continuing.
